title: Dog Scrotal Redness and Swelling: Causes and Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog's scrotum red and swollen? Learn about common causes, urgency levels, and why a photo is essential for a veterinary triage assessment.
content_html:
TL;DR: Scrotal redness and swelling in dogs usually indicate inflammation or infection and require a veterinary exam within 24–48 hours; use an Elizabethan collar immediately to prevent self-trauma.

What causes my dog's scrotum to become red and swollen?

Noticing that your dog's scrotal skin is red, inflamed, or enlarged can be very worrying. This condition often indicates irritation or inflammation of the skin (dermatitis) or the underlying reproductive tissues. Common causes include allergic reactions to environmental triggers like grass or cleaning chemicals, bacterial or fungal infections, insect bites, or even trauma. In more serious cases, it may represent orchitis (inflammation of the testicles) or epididymitis, which can be quite painful for your pet.

Is scrotal swelling in dogs considered a medical emergency?

  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours, as this is generally a medium-urgency concern.
  • Place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your dog immediately to stop them from licking or biting the area, which prevents the condition from worsening.
  • Contact an emergency clinic right away if your dog is vomiting, lethargic, has a fever, or is in visible distress.
  • Monitor your pet closely, as while rarely an immediate life-threat, the condition is very uncomfortable and risks spreading infection.

How does providing a photo help with my dog's triage?

A clear, well-lit photo of the affected area is a vital tool for veterinary triage. It allows a professional to visualize the exact shade of redness, the extent of the swelling, and the presence of any discharge or ulceration. Because this area is sensitive, a photo can sometimes provide more information than a physical description alone, helping the vet determine if your dog needs to be squeezed into an emergency slot or if the issue can wait for a standard appointment.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Acute inflammation of the testis or epididymis in dogs can result in scrotal redness and swelling, caused by trauma, infection (fungal, bacterial, or viral), or testicular torsion. Infection can be hematogenous or urologic in origin. Clinical signs include pain and swelling of the testes, epididymides, and/or scrotum, and there may be wounds or other lesions in the scrotal skin. Diagnostic evaluation should include careful palpation of the scrotal contents, although patient discomfort and edema can make this difficult. Ultrasonography, performed with sedation or analgesia as needed, is helpful to further evaluate the affected structures and confirm the presence of testicular torsion and focal lesions such as masses or abscesses. Diagnostic tests should also include evaluation for Brucella canis infection, and cytologic examination of semen with bacterial and mycoplasmal culture may be helpful, although semen collection may be difficult in ill or painful animals. Testicular or epididymal specimens for cytology and culture may be obtained by fine-needle aspiration, and testicular biopsy for histopathology and bacterial culture may be performed if needed after less invasive diagnostic tests.

Chapter: Urology, Infectious Disease, Surgery

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1400)

faqs.question: Can I apply over-the-counter human ointments to my dog's scrotum?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid applying any human medications or ointments unless specifically directed by a veterinarian. Many products contain ingredients that are toxic if licked or may cause further irritation to the sensitive scrotal skin.

faqs.question: Could my dog's scrotal swelling be caused by an allergy?
faqs.answer: Yes, contact dermatitis is a common cause. Dogs can react to chemicals used on carpets, fertilizers in the grass, or even certain laundry detergents used on their bedding.

faqs.question: Is scrotal swelling common after a neuter surgery?
faqs.answer: A small amount of swelling can be normal immediately following surgery, but significant redness, bruising, or a 'firm' feel to the scrotum should be reported to your surgeon immediately as it may indicate a hematoma or infection.
